title: SEGMENT 8 — The CCS / HiFi paradigm shift: multiple passes, error correction, 99.9% accuracy

lines.text: SEGMENT 8 — The CCS / HiFi paradigm shift: multiple passes, error correction, 99.9% accuracy
So we've now arrived at the most important conceptual moment in the whole history of PacBio: the circular consensus sequencing workflow, or CCS, also called HiFi in the modern PacBio branding.
This is the change that, in 2019 and 2020, took PacBio from being a niche specialty platform to being the gold standard for serious genome assembly.
I want to spend this whole segment on it, because if you understand only one thing about PacBio sequencing in the modern era, it should be this.
Let me set the stage.
For most of PacBio's history, the platform produced what were called continuous long reads, or CLR.
The polymerase would read across the SMRTbell once or twice, generating a long, single-pass read of maybe ten or twenty kilobases.
The accuracy of these CLR reads was poor — somewhere between eighty-five and ninety percent base accuracy.

That's the raw error rate of single-molecule sequencing chemistry: about ten to fifteen percent of bases are wrong, either substitutions, insertions, or — most commonly — deletions, where the polymerase added a base but the camera missed the pulse.
Eighty-five percent accuracy sounds terrible.
It is, by Illumina standards.
Illumina reads are around ninety-nine point nine percent accurate per base, raw, no correction needed.
Comparing on a per-base basis, PacBio CLR was a thousand times worse than Illumina.
For many years, this was the standard rap against PacBio — "PacBio reads are too noisy to use directly.
But — and here's the original PacBio insight — even with a fifteen percent per-base error rate, the errors are essentially random.
They happen at random positions, with random identities.
If you can read the same molecule many times and align the reads, the errors cancel out and the true sequence emerges.

You can either align across multiple molecules (which is what you do when assembling a genome — overlap many independent reads and consensus the result), or you can align across multiple passes of the same molecule (which is what CCS does using the SMRTbell).
Either way, the consensus accuracy is way better than the raw accuracy.
For years, PacBio's strategy was the "many independent molecules" approach.
You'd run a sample on PacBio, get a bunch of long noisy reads, and use them to construct a genome assembly by finding overlaps.
The assembly itself would be highly accurate even though individual reads were not, because each genomic position was covered by, say, fifty different reads, and the consensus was reliable.
This worked, but it required heavy sequencing — you needed lots of coverage to overcome the per-read noise — and the noisy reads were hard to use for any application that didn't involve assembly.
You couldn't really use a single PacBio CLR read for, say, accurate variant calling, because the read itself was too unreliable.
Then in 2019, PacBio rolled out the CCS workflow as a major product, and renamed the output HiFi reads.
The idea was: instead of optimizing for read length — which had been the obsession of the previous decade — optimize for the number of passes.
Use a slightly shorter insert, say fifteen to twenty kilobases instead of fifty.
Let the polymerase read around it many times, accumulating many passes per molecule.
Then run a consensus algorithm on those passes, in software, to produce a single corrected read.
The math works out beautifully.

With ten or more passes, the consensus accuracy reaches ninety-nine point nine percent — that's Q30 quality, equivalent to Illumina, but on a read that's fifteen to twenty kilobases long instead of one hundred fifty bases.
With twenty or more passes, you can hit ninety-nine point ninety-nine percent — Q40, better than Illumina.
Long and accurate, in a single read, from a single molecule.
The headline here is worth saying as clearly as I can: HiFi reads are long-read and high-accuracy at the same time, for the first time in sequencing history.
Until HiFi, there was always a tradeoff.
Illumina: short and accurate.
Nanopore CLR-style: long and noisy.
PacBio CLR: long and noisy too.
You picked one or the other; you couldn't have both.
With HiFi, that tradeoff collapses.
You get fifteen to twenty kilobases of read length, and you get Q30 to Q40 quality at every base.
It's the long-read holy grail.
This is genuinely a paradigm shift, and the field's reaction over the following few years was to reorganize itself around it.
Major genome assembly projects rebuilt their pipelines for HiFi.

The first true telomere-to-telomere human genome assembly, T2T-CHM13 — the one that filled in those missing eight percent of the human reference — was driven by PacBio HiFi data, along with Oxford Nanopore data.
The Vertebrate Genomes Project, building reference-quality assemblies for thousands of species, standardized on HiFi.
Clinical sequencing labs that had been Illumina-only started adopting HiFi for cases where structural variation mattered.
Within about two years, HiFi had become the de facto standard for serious de novo assembly.
Let me give you the analogy I want you to remember for this.
A raw PacBio read is like a single hand-copy of a manuscript by a tired scribe — about fifteen percent of the words are slightly wrong.
A HiFi read is what you get when you ask twenty different tired scribes to each independently copy the same manuscript, and then you compare their copies side by side.
The places where they all agree are almost certainly right.
The places where they disagree, you use majority vote, and you're almost certainly right there too.
Twenty noisy copies, one accurate consensus.
The SMRTbell gives you the twenty copies.
The CCS algorithm gives you the consensus.
The output is a HiFi read.
Long.
Accurate.
Single-molecule.
No PCR.
The product of a beautifully integrated chain of physics, chemistry, biology, and software, with the final accuracy coming from statistical aggregation over many passes of the same molecule.
Two more things I want to mention about HiFi before we move on.
First — HiFi reads are sometimes mislabeled as "consensus reads" in a way that suggests they come from averaging many different molecules.
They don't.
They come from averaging many passes of the same molecule.
The distinction is crucial for downstream applications.
A consensus across molecules averages away true single-molecule properties — for instance, methylation, which can differ from one molecule to another.
A consensus across passes of one molecule preserves those properties, because every pass is sampling the same physical molecule.

PacBio HiFi data, therefore, carries methylation information for every read, which is something Illumina simply cannot do (because of PCR amplification) and which Nanopore can do but in a slightly different way.
Second — the move to HiFi was, internally at PacBio, a major bet.
For years the platform's marketing had emphasized read length, and the engineering had been pushed in that direction.
Switching to shorter inserts to get more passes per molecule was a strategic reversal.
But it was the right call.
The combination of long-and-accurate has turned out to be more valuable than long-and-noisy, for almost every application.
The HiFi era of PacBio is, in essence, the company finally finding its market fit, fifteen years after founding.
Now let's look at what those reads actually look like in practice — the read length distribution, the throughput, what you do with them.
